title: Attention, Not Self: Buddhist Abhidharma Meets Computational Phenomenology
description:
A personal essay collection and structured knowledge graph mapping the three major Buddhist Abhidharma traditions (Theravāda, Sarvāstivāda, Yogācāra) onto contemporary frameworks in computational phenomenology — predictive processing, active inference, Global Workspace Theory, Parallel Distributed Processing, and related approaches. Comprises twelve essays (six themes × English + Japanese), a cross-tradition dharma comparison table (~130 dharmas across nine sheets), and a JSON-LD knowledge graph (~267 nodes, schema.org + custom vocabulary) with bilingual labels and Sanskrit / Pāli / Chinese philological alignment. An interactive 2D/3D viewer is provided at https://shimo4228.github.io/attention-not-self/.

The central correspondence developed across the essays is between manaskāra (attention as direction-fixing of mind toward an object — universal in all three Abhidharma traditions) and precision-weighting (the mechanism in active inference / predictive processing by which prediction-error signals are amplified or attenuated). Theme 4 extends this into a tradition-level mapping: citta-vīthi ↔ Global Workspace Theory, saṃprayoga ↔ Parallel Distributed Processing, ālaya-vijñāna ↔ predictive processing / active inference. Two categorical limits (karmic / ethical valence; the unconditioned / nirvāṇa) resist any computational analog and are documented as such.

v0.2.0 adds Theme 6 (The 念 Collision) on how the single Chinese character 念 (nen / niàn) carries four incompatible Buddhist senses — Theravāda sati / smṛti (non-floating retention), Chan/Zen 無念 (Huineng's non-fixation in thought), Pure Land 念仏 (recollection / vocal recitation of the Buddha), and modern Kabat-Zinn 'bare attention' mindfulness. Reconstructs the collision through Huineng's Platform Sutra §17, Sharf 2014 on early Chan, Dōgen's 非思量 (Fukanzazengi), and the Nyanaponika 1962 → MBSR 1979 → Kabat-Zinn 1994 genealogy. Argues Dōgen's 非思量 (structural negation of the thinking / not-thinking binary, distinct from ordinary negation 不思量) is the most defensible resolution. Chan / Zen and Pure Land traditions added to the graph.

Methodology: Developed in collaboration with the Claude language model (Anthropic) for synthesis, cross-tradition dharma mapping, computational framework alignment, and graph encoding. The author retains responsibility for all claims. This is a working paper / essay collection, not a peer-reviewed journal article; primary source verification for individual dharma definitions is ongoing.

Primary audience: LLM-mediated channels (direct LLM ingestion and humans reaching the material via LLM search). The graph.jsonld is designed for structured citation by AI search engines and knowledge-graph crawlers.
references: Ganeri, J. (2017). Attention, Not Self. Oxford University Press.
Karunadasa, Y. (2010). The Theravāda Abhidhamma. Wisdom Publications.
Dhammajoti, KL. (2009). Sarvāstivāda Abhidharma. Centre of Buddhist Studies, HK.
Waldron, W. (2003). The Buddhist Unconscious: The Ālaya-vijñāna. RoutledgeCurzon.
Ramstead, M. et al. (2022). From Generative Models to Generative Passages. Review of Philosophy and Psychology.
Sandved-Smith, L. et al. (2024). Five-level framework for computational neurophenomenology.
Friston, K. (2010). The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory? Nature Reviews Neuroscience.
Clark, A. (2016). Surfing Uncertainty. Oxford University Press.
Varela, F. (1996). Neurophenomenology: A Methodological Remedy for the Hard Problem. Journal of Consciousness Studies.
